one_line_takeaway: TC-UMIA shows that machine-unlearning models leak population-level membership information not just about the forgotten data but, often more strongly, about the retained data, and that dropout gives the best privacy/utility trade-off among the three defenses tested (label-only output, dropout, DP-SGD).
key_finding: On ResNet-18, TC-UMIA's overall (micro-F1) attack accuracy ranges from 56.88% (GA/CINIC-10) up to 95.6% (SISA/TinyImageNet, with per-class F1 of 95.99% unseen, 96.53% forget, 94.32% retain), and it beats both baselines in every reported configuration -- e.g., for SISA/TinyImageNet, U-Leak scores 80.91% overall and the Two-round Attack scores 70.97% overall. Averaged across settings, TC-UMIA improves overall F1 by up to 22.7% over the best single-model attack; on the Pythia-70M language model it averages a 9.3% improvement over U-Leak and 22.56% over the Two-round Attack. TC-UMIA usually attains its highest per-class accuracy on the retain set and lowest on the forget set, attributed to forget-set posteriors not changing much after unlearning (especially when only a small fraction of data is removed), making forget-set features resemble retain-set features. TC-UMIA is more effective against exact unlearning (Retrain, SISA) than approximate methods (Sparsity, SCRUB, GA), because approximate unlearning leaves residual influence of the forget set. TC-UMIA degrades only moderately under restricted outputs (predicted-label-only accuracy of 69.23% vs. 77.19% with the full posterior vector, on SimpleCNN/CINIC-10). For defenses (evaluated on SimpleCNN, retraining as the unlearning method): all three (label-only output, dropout p=95%, DP-SGD with epsilon in {2,5}) reduce attack accuracy relative to no defense (e.g., CIFAR-10 overall attack accuracy: 62.87% no-defense to 53.33% label-only, 43.93% dropout, 34.60% DP-eps=5, 33.71% DP-eps=2), with DP giving the strongest privacy protection but the largest utility loss (CIFAR-10 test accuracy drops from 68.03% to 49.77%/47.01% under DP-eps=5/2, vs. 68.03% unchanged under label-only and 63.22% under dropout). The paper concludes dropout offers the best privacy-utility trade-off, and the same pattern (DP strongest but costliest, dropout best balance) is replicated on the Pythia-70M/SST5 language-model setting.
core_contribution: The paper extends membership-inference privacy analysis of machine unlearning (MU) beyond the forget set to also cover the retain set (data that stays in the model). It formalizes a 'tri-class unlearning membership inference game' (unseen vs. forget vs. retain) and proposes TC-UMIA, described as the first black-box tri-class unlearning membership inference attack (U-MIA). TC-UMIA trains shadow original/unlearned models, derives attack features from the ground-truth-label posterior probabilities before and after unlearning (two feature variants: ConProb = concatenation Py||P-y, and ConSumDif = concatenation of (Py-P-y) and (Py+P-y)), and trains a 3-class MLP classifier (32-16-3 neurons) to infer whether a queried sample belongs to the unseen, forget, or retain set. The paper also evaluates three adapted defenses (label-only output, dropout, DP-SGD) against TC-UMIA, and includes extensions/discussions: an example-level variant (TC-ULiRA), attacks under limited/label-only output, and robustness to the type of removed sample (uncertainty, per-sample privacy vulnerability, outlierness).
threat_model: MLaaS setting: a server hosts an original model f (trained on D) and, after an unlearning request, an unlearned model f- (obtained by removing forget set D_F from f). The server continues to expose black-box query access to both models (posterior probability vectors). The adversary has black-box access only (no knowledge of model architecture, hyperparameters, or which unlearning algorithm was used), possesses a shadow dataset whose distribution may differ from the target's training distribution, and cannot poison the training/forget process. The adversary's goal is to classify a queried sample (x,y) into one of three membership classes -- unseen (D_U), forget (D_F), or retain (D_R = D\D_F) -- for both f and f- jointly, formalized as an indistinguishability game with b in {0,1,2}. This is an attack-driven (not evaluation-driven) threat model, explicitly contrasted in the paper with prior evaluation-driven U-MIAs that assume white-box, worst-case adversaries.
limitations: The authors themselves flag several: (1) TC-UMIA operates only at the population level; an example-level extension (TC-ULiRA, sketched via a Gaussian-likelihood algorithm in Section 9.1) is presented only as a discussion/algorithm, not a fully evaluated attack, and is noted to incur substantial computational cost, making it less practical. (2) Evaluation on language models is limited to a single small model (Pythia-70M, 70M parameters) on two short-text classification datasets; the authors explicitly discuss (Section 9.3) that extending TC-UMIA to large generative LLMs raises unresolved technical challenges (token-level likelihood shifts, high member/non-member n-gram overlap degrading MIA performance) and leave this to future work. (3) TC-UMIA's forget-set detection accuracy is consistently its weakest per-class metric, particularly against approximate unlearning algorithms that leave residual forget-set influence. (4) The threat model assumes the adversary cannot control or poison the composition of the forget set (a randomly-selected forget set), which the authors acknowledge is a more realistic but less worst-case setting than some evaluation-driven attacks. (5) The evaluated defenses are adaptations of existing generic MIA defenses (label-only, dropout, DP-SGD), not defenses purpose-built for the tri-class setting; the paper explicitly proposes 'tailored defenses' (e.g., overfitting-suppressing regularizers) as future work (Section 9.5) rather than something it evaluates.

quantitative_results: TC-UMIA overall micro-F1 on ResNet-18/SISA/TinyImageNet: 95.6% (per-class F1: 95.99% unseen, 96.53% forget, 94.32% retain)
TC-UMIA lowest overall F1 across ResNet-18 configs: 56.88% (GA/CINIC-10)
TC-UMIA improves up to 22.7% overall F1 over best single-model attack; 9.3% over U-Leak and 22.56% over Two-round Attack on Pythia-70M (averages)
TC-UMIA vs U-Leak vs Two-round Attack overall F1 (Retrain/CIFAR-100): 79.98% vs 62.11% vs 56.98%
TC-UMIA with predicted-label-only output: 69.23% overall (vs 77.19% with full posterior vector), SimpleCNN/CINIC-10
Defense results (CIFAR-10, SimpleCNN, retraining): overall attack accuracy 62.87% (no defense) -> 53.33% (label-only) -> 43.93% (dropout) -> 34.60% (DP eps=5) -> 33.71% (DP eps=2)
Model test accuracy under defenses (CIFAR-10): 68.03% (no defense / label-only, unchanged) -> 63.22% (dropout) -> 49.77% (DP eps=5) -> 47.01% (DP eps=2)

Language model (Pythia-70M/SST5) defense results: attack accuracy 73.68% (no defense) -> 66.27% (label-only) -> 49.74% (dropout) -> 34.12% (DP eps=5) -> 29.37% (DP eps=2); test accuracy drops from 46.33% (no defense/label-only) to 27.76% (DP eps=2)
baselines_compared: Two-round Attack (independent ML-leaks-based binary MIA against original and unlearned models, combined via fixed decision rule)
U-Leak (adapted from Chen et al.'s attack-driven U-MIA, originally binary forget-vs-unseen, relabeled to tri-class setting, best of 5 feature variants reported)
